subject: AP Physics C: Mechanics
source: AP Physics C: Mechanics 2012 Practice Exam
text:
Experiment 1. A block of mass $0.30\,\mathrm{kg}$ is placed on a frictionless table and is attached to one end of a horizontal spring of spring constant $k$, as shown above. The other end of the spring is attached to a fixed wall. The block is set into oscillatory motion by stretching the spring and releasing the block from rest at time $t=0$. A motion detector is used to record the position of the block as it oscillates. The resulting graph of velocity $v$ versus time $t$ is shown below. The positive direction for all quantities is to the right.

(a) Determine the equation for $v(t)$, including numerical values for all constants.

(b) Given that the equilibrium position is at $x=0$, determine the equation for $x(t)$, including numerical values for all constants.

(c) Calculate the value of $k$.

Experiment 2. The block and spring arrangement is now placed on a rough surface, as shown below. The block is displaced so that the spring is compressed a distance $d$ and released from rest.

(d) On the dots below that represent the block, draw and label the forces, not components, that act on the block when the spring is compressed a distance $x=d/2$ and the block is moving in the direction indicated below each dot.

(e) Draw a sketch of $v$ versus $t$ in this case. Assume that there is a negligible change in the period and that the positive direction is still to the right.

points.description: Part (a): Award 1 point for writing a correct trigonometric equation for velocity as a function of time, including the correct negative sign or phase, such as $v(t)=-v_{\max}\sin(\omega t)$. Award 1 point for using $\omega=2\pi f$ or $\omega=2\pi/T$ to solve for angular frequency. Award 1 point for using the correct period $T=0.70\,\mathrm{s}$ from the graph, giving $\omega=2\pi/(0.70\,\mathrm{s})=9.0\,\mathrm{rad/s}$. Award 1 point for using a maximum speed from the graph in the acceptable range $0.15\,\mathrm{m/s}$ to $0.17\,\mathrm{m/s}$, for example $v(t)=-(0.16\,\mathrm{m/s})\sin(9.0t)$. Deduct 1 point for an incorrect phase shift. Full credit is possible for a correct answer with no work shown.

Part (b): Award 1 point for a correct trigonometric expression consistent with integrating the velocity from part (a), for example $x(t)=\int v(t)\,dt$. Award 1 point for a correct maximum displacement consistent with the integration, $x_{\max}=(0.16\,\mathrm{m/s})/(9.0\,\mathrm{rad/s})=0.018\,\mathrm{m}$, giving $x(t)=(0.018\,\mathrm{m})\cos(9.0t)$ for the stated initial condition. Full credit is possible for a correct answer with no work shown; deduct one earned point for incorrect initial conditions such as subtracting an inappropriate constant from the cosine function.

Part (c): Award 1 point for a correct relationship between period and spring constant, $T=2\pi\sqrt{m/k}$, or equivalently $\omega=\sqrt{k/m}$. Award 1 point for substituting correct values from earlier parts, such as $k=4\pi^2m/T^2=4\pi^2(0.30\,\mathrm{kg})/(0.70\,\mathrm{s})^2$, or $k=m\omega^2=(0.30\,\mathrm{kg})(9.0\,\mathrm{rad/s})^2$, giving $k=24\,\mathrm{N/m}$. A conservation of energy solution using $\frac12kx_{\max}^2=\frac12mv_{\max}^2$ with consistent values also earns full credit.

Part (d): Award 1 point for drawing and labeling $F_N$ and $mg$ correctly on both free-body diagrams. For the diagram with the block moving toward equilibrium, award 1 point for a correctly drawn and labeled spring force to the right and 1 point for a correctly drawn and labeled friction force to the left. For the diagram with the block moving away from equilibrium, award 1 point for correctly drawn and labeled spring and friction forces both to the right. Vector length is not considered, only direction. There is a maximum 1-point deduction for extraneous forces or for vectors not touching or almost touching the dot.

Part (e): Award 1 point for a velocity-time graph that passes through equilibrium at $0.35\,\mathrm{s}$ intervals, consistent with the original period. Award 1 point for showing damped oscillations. Award 1 point for a graph that starts at zero velocity and then increases in the positive direction.

subject: AP Physics C: Mechanics
source: AP Physics C: Mechanics 2012 Practice Exam
text:
You are to perform an experiment investigating the conservation of mechanical energy involving a transformation from initial gravitational potential energy to translational kinetic energy.

(a) You are given the equipment listed below, all the supports required to hold the equipment, and a lab table. On the list below, indicate each piece of equipment you would use by checking the line next to each item.

- [ ] Track
- [ ] Meterstick
- [ ] Set of objects of different masses
- [ ] Cart
- [ ] Electronic balance
- [ ] Lightweight low-friction pulley
- [ ] String
- [ ] Stopwatch

(b) Outline a procedure for performing the experiment. Include a diagram of your experimental setup. Label the equipment in your diagram. Also include a description of the measurements you would make and a symbol for each measurement.

(c) Give a detailed account of the calculations of gravitational potential energy and translational kinetic energy both before and after the transformation, in terms of the quantities measured in part (b).

(d) After your first trial, your calculations show that the energy increased during the experiment. Assuming you made no mathematical errors, give a reasonable explanation for this result.

(e) On all other trials, your calculations show that the energy decreased during the experiment. Assuming you made no mathematical errors, give a reasonable physical explanation for the fact that the average energy you determined decreased. Include references to conservative and nonconservative forces, as appropriate.

points.description: Part (a): Award 1 point for choosing the meterstick and stopwatch, regardless of what else is checked.

Part (b): Award 1 point for a procedure that indicates the height needed to calculate gravitational potential energy; 1 point for a procedure that indicates distance and time measurements needed to calculate velocity; 1 point for a diagram with a clear indication of the height measurement; and 1 point for a diagram with a clear indication of the distance measurement. Acceptable setups include a cart attached by a string over a pulley to a hanging object, measuring the fall distance $h$, table height $H$, and time $t$, or an inclined track setup measuring height $h$, track distance $d$, and time $t$.

Part (c): Award 1 point for a clear indication of the initial gravitational potential energy of the system, 1 point for a clear indication of the final gravitational potential energy, 1 point for a clear indication of the initial kinetic energy, and 1 point for a clear indication of the final kinetic energy. Award 2 points for a correct calculation of the instantaneous velocity of the system. In the pulley example, acceptable expressions include initial gravitational potential energy $U_i=m_cgH+m_og h$, final gravitational potential energy $U_f=m_cgH$, initial kinetic energy $K_i=0$, final kinetic energy $K_f=\frac12(m_c+m_o)v_f^2$, and constant-acceleration kinematics such as $d=\frac12(v_i+v_f)t$ giving $v_f=2h/t$ for the falling distance. In the incline example, acceptable expressions include $U_i=mgh$, $U_f=0$, $K_i=0$, $K_f=\frac12mv_f^2$, and $v_f=2d/t$.

Part (d): Award 1 point for identifying a reasonable cause for the apparent increase in energy and 1 point for a reasonable explanation connected to that cause. An acceptable example is that an unintentional push was applied to the cart, adding energy to the system.

Part (e): Award 1 point for identifying a reasonable cause for the decrease in energy related to nonconservative forces acting on the system and 1 point for a reasonable explanation connected to that cause. An acceptable example is that friction acts on the cart or object, reducing the speed and converting mechanical energy into thermal energy.

subject: AP Physics C: Mechanics
source: AP Physics C: Mechanics 2012 Practice Exam
text:
A ring of mass $M$, radius $R$, and rotational inertia $MR^2$ is initially sliding on a frictionless surface at constant velocity $v_0$ to the right, as shown above. At time $t=0$ it encounters a surface with coefficient of friction $\mu$ and begins sliding and rotating. After traveling a distance $L$, the ring begins rolling without sliding. Express all answers in terms of $M$, $R$, $v_0$, $\mu$, and fundamental constants, as appropriate.

(a) Starting from Newton's second law in either translational or rotational form, as appropriate, derive a differential equation that can be used to solve for the magnitude of each of the following as the ring is sliding and rotating.

  i. The linear velocity $v$ of the ring as a function of time $t$

  ii. The angular velocity $\omega$ of the ring as a function of time $t$

(b) Derive an expression for the magnitude of each of the following as the ring is sliding and rotating.

  i. The linear velocity $v$ of the ring as a function of time $t$

  ii. The angular velocity $\omega$ of the ring as a function of time $t$

(c) Derive an expression for the time it takes the ring to travel the distance $L$.

(d) Derive an expression for the magnitude of the velocity of the ring immediately after it has traveled the distance $L$.

(e) Derive an expression for the distance $L$.
unit_name: Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems

topics.name: Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems

points.description: Part (a)(i): Award 1 point for starting with Newton's second law for translation with friction as the net force, $\sum F=-f=Ma$. Award 1 point for a correct expression for the frictional force, $f=\mu Mg$. Award 1 point for indicating that linear acceleration is the time derivative of velocity, $a=dv/dt$, giving $dv/dt=-\mu g$.

Part (a)(ii): Award 1 point for starting with Newton's second law for rotation with the correct rotational inertia, $\tau=MR^2\alpha$. Award 1 point for a correct torque expression using friction, $\tau=\mu MgR$. Award 1 point for indicating that angular acceleration is the time derivative of angular velocity, $\alpha=d\omega/dt$, giving $d\omega/dt=\mu g/R$.

Part (b)(i): Award 1 point for setting up the integral of the function from part (a)(i), $\int_{v_0}^{v}dv=-\int_0^t\mu g\,dt$. Award 1 point for the correct result $v=v_0-\mu gt$. An alternate kinematics solution using $a=-\mu g$ and $v=v_0+at$ earns the same points.

Part (b)(ii): Award 1 point for setting up the integral of the function from part (a)(ii), $\int_0^\omega d\omega=\int_0^t(\mu g/R)\,dt$. Award 1 point for the correct result $\omega=\mu gt/R$. An alternate rotational-kinematics solution using $\alpha=\mu g/R$ and $\omega=\omega_0+\alpha t$ earns the same points.

Part (c): Award 1 point for indicating that the linear speed equals $R\omega$ when slipping stops, $v=R\omega$. Substituting the results from part (b), $v_0-\mu gt=R(\mu gt/R)$. Award 1 point for the correct result $t=v_0/(2\mu g)$.
Part (d): Award 1 point for substituting the time from part (c) into a correct kinematics equation, $v=v_0-\mu g(v_0/(2\mu g))$, giving $v=v_0/2$.

Part (e): Award 1 point for setting up the integral of the velocity function from part (b)(i), $L=\int_0^t(v_0-\mu gt)\,dt$. Award 1 point for the correct result with supporting work, $L=[v_0t-\frac12\mu gt^2]_0^{v_0/(2\mu g)}=3v_0^2/(8\mu g)$. Equivalent kinematics solutions using $v^2=v_0^2+2a\Delta x$ with $v=v_0/2$ or $\Delta x=v_0t+\frac12at^2$ with $t=v_0/(2\mu g)$ earn the same points.

subject: AP Physics C: Mechanics
source: AP Physics C: Mechanics 2013 Practice Exam
text:
A ball of mass $m$ and cross-sectional area $A$ is released from rest near the surface of Earth. The ball experiences a resistive force due to the air that is proportional to the ball's velocity, $F_R=bAv$, where $b$ is a positive constant. Determine all algebraic answers in terms of $m$, $b$, $A$, and fundamental constants.

(a) Draw free-body diagrams for the ball for the following situations. Give each vector a descriptive label and draw them approximately to scale.

- A short time after the ball is released
- Once the ball has reached terminal velocity

(b) Calculate the terminal velocity $v_T$ of the ball.

(c) Starting from Newton's laws, derive an expression for the time required for the ball to reach one third the terminal velocity.

(d) Sketch graphs of the ball's acceleration and velocity from when the ball is released at time $t=0$ until it approaches terminal velocity.

points.description: Part (a): Award 1 point for having all four forces properly labeled and in the proper direction on the two diagrams. Award 1 point for having both weight vectors $W$ of equal length. Award 1 point for showing $F_R<W$ a short time after the ball is released. Award 1 point for showing $F_R=W$ at terminal velocity. Deduct 1 earned point if there are extraneous forces.
Part (b): Award 1 point for setting the resistive force equal to the weight at terminal velocity, $R=W$, and substituting $bAv_T=mg$. Award 1 point for the correct magnitude $v_T=mg/(bA)$.

Part (c): Award 1 point for a correct differential equation from Newton's second law, $F=ma=mg-bAv$, so $m\,dv/dt=mg-bAv$. Award 1 point for a correct expression for velocity as a function of time, such as $v(t)=\frac{mg}{bA}(1-e^{-bAt/m})$. Award 1 point for substituting $v_T/3$ into the expression. Award 1 point for the correct time, $t=-(m/(bA))\ln(2/3)=(m/(bA))\ln(3/2)$. If the differential equation is incorrect, a student can still earn credit for correctly solving the equation and substituting.

Part (d): Award 1 point for an acceleration graph that does not begin at the origin; 1 point for an acceleration graph that has a horizontal asymptote at $a=0$; 1 point for a velocity graph that begins at the origin; 1 point for a velocity graph that has a horizontal asymptote at $v=v_T$ or $-v_T$ as appropriate; and 1 point for both graphs having proper and accurate curvature.

subject: AP Physics C: Mechanics
source: AP Physics C: Mechanics 2013 Practice Exam
text:
A $0.30\,\mathrm{kg}$ cart moving at $2.0\,\mathrm{m/s}$ to the right collides with a $0.10\,\mathrm{kg}$ cart, initially moving at $3.0\,\mathrm{m/s}$ to the left. After the collision, the $0.10\,\mathrm{kg}$ cart moves to the right with a speed of $1.2\,\mathrm{m/s}$. Assume friction is negligible.

(a) Calculate the magnitude and direction of the velocity of the $0.30\,\mathrm{kg}$ cart after the collision.

(b) Is this an elastic collision? Justify your answer.

The $0.30\,\mathrm{kg}$ cart is now removed. The $0.10\,\mathrm{kg}$ cart continues to move to the right and then collides with and compresses an ideal spring whose right end is attached to a fixed wall.

(c) Calculate the potential energy of the spring when it reaches maximum compression.

(d) The maximum compression of the spring is $0.10\,\mathrm{m}$. Calculate the spring constant of the spring.

(e) Suppose instead that the spring is nonlinear such that the force $F$ due to the spring when it is compressed a distance $x$ is given by $F=bx^3$. Calculate the value of the constant $b$ if the spring is compressed $0.10\,\mathrm{m}$.

points.description: Part (a): Award 1 point for a correct conservation of momentum statement, $p_i=p_f$ or $m_1v_{1i}+m_2v_{2i}=m_1v_{1f}+m_2v_{2f}$. Award 1 point for correct substitution, $(0.30)(2.0)+(0.10)(-3.0)=(0.30)v_{1f}+(0.10)(1.2)$. Award 1 point for the correct speed with units, $v_{1f}=0.60\,\mathrm{m/s}$. Award 1 point for correctly indicating the direction is to the right.

Part (b): Award 1 point for answering no, or that the collision is inelastic. Award 1 point for a correct justification, such as showing the final kinetic energy is less than the initial kinetic energy. For example, $K_i=\frac12(0.30)(2.0)^2+\frac12(0.10)(3.0)^2=1.05\,\mathrm{J}$, while $K_f=\frac12(0.30)(0.60)^2+\frac12(0.10)(1.2)^2=0.126\,\mathrm{J}$. A justification based on reduced relative speed is also acceptable.

Part (c): Award 1 point for a correct conservation of energy statement, such as $K_i=U_{sp}$ or $\frac12mv^2=U_s$. Award 1 point for correct substitution, $U_s=\frac12(0.10\,\mathrm{kg})(1.2\,\mathrm{m/s})^2$. Award 1 point for the correct answer with units, $U_s=0.072\,\mathrm{J}$. If the wrong cart is used or mass/velocity values are mixed, only the first point can be earned.

Part (d): Award 1 point for a correct spring energy expression, $U=\frac12kx^2$. Award 1 point for an answer consistent with part (c), using $0.072\,\mathrm{J}=\frac12k(0.10\,\mathrm{m})^2$. Award 1 point for correct units. The expected result is $k=14.4\,\mathrm{N/m}$; $10.8\,\mathrm{N/m}$ is acceptable if the wrong cart was consistently used in part (c).

Part (e): Award 1 point for setting up a correct integral, $U=\int F\,dx=\int bx^3\,dx$. Award 1 point for a correct equation for the spring potential energy, $U_s=\frac14bx^4$. Award 1 point for an answer consistent with part (c), with correct sign and units: $0.072=\frac14b(0.10\,\mathrm{m})^4$, giving $b=2880\,\mathrm{N/m^3}$. If the wrong cart was used in part (c), $b=2160\,\mathrm{N/m^3}$ is acceptable.

subject: AP Physics C: Mechanics
source: AP Physics C: Mechanics 2013 Practice Exam
text:
A solid disk of radius $0.10\,\mathrm{m}$ is mounted on a vertical axis. A string of negligible mass is wrapped around the rim of the disk, passes over a small, lightweight, frictionless pulley as shown above, and is tied to a block of mass $0.05\,\mathrm{kg}$. The system is released from rest, and a computer records the velocity of the falling block as a function of time, as shown below.

(a) Using the graph above, calculate the acceleration of the falling block.

(b) Use your result from part (a) to calculate the rotational inertia of the disk.

(c) Calculate the angular momentum of the disk at time $t=0.45\,\mathrm{s}$.

(d) The disk is removed and replaced with a hoop of the same mass and radius, but with all of its mass concentrated near the rim of the hoop and connected to the axis by lightweight spokes. The experiment is then repeated. Is the angular acceleration of the hoop greater than, less than, or the same as that of the solid disk?

- [ ] Greater than
- [ ] Less than
- [ ] The same

Justify your answer.
unit_name: Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems

topics.name: Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems

points.description: Part (a): Award 1 point for correctly expressing acceleration as the slope or rate of change of velocity, $a=\Delta v/\Delta t$. Award 1 point for using two points from a best-fit line to calculate the slope, not data points unless the data points are on the line, for example $a=(1.52-0.71)\,\mathrm{m/s}/((0.55-0.25)\,\mathrm{s})$. Award 1 point for a reasonable numerical value; acceptable values are about $2.5$ to $3.3\,\mathrm{m/s^2}$. Award 1 point for correct units.

Part (b): Award 1 point for a correct Newton's second law expression for rotational motion, $\tau=I\alpha$ or $TR=I\alpha$. Award 1 point for substituting angular acceleration in terms of linear acceleration, $\alpha=a/R$, so $TR=I(a/R)$. Award 1 point for a correct translational Newton's second law expression, $mg-T=ma$, so $T=m(g-a)$. Combining gives $I=TR/\alpha=m(g-a)R^2/a$. Award 1 point for a numerical answer consistent with part (a), for example $I=(0.05)(9.8-2.7)(0.10)^2/(2.7)=0.0013\,\mathrm{kg\cdot m^2}$. Award 1 point for correct units.

Part (c): Award 1 point for a correct angular momentum expression, $L=I\omega$. Award 1 point for correct substitution into a rotational kinematics expression, such as $\omega=\alpha t$ with $\alpha=a/R$, or $\omega=v/R$ with $v=at$, giving $L=Iat/R$. Award 1 point for a numerical answer consistent with parts (a) and (b), for example $L=(0.0013)(2.7)(0.45)/(0.10)=0.016\,\mathrm{kg\cdot m^2/s}$. Award 1 point for correct units. An alternate solution using $L=Iv/R$ and the graph value of $v$ at $t=0.45\,\mathrm{s}$ is acceptable.

Part (d): Award 1 point for correctly selecting "Less than." Award 1 point for a correct justification. Acceptable explanations include that the same force or torque acts, but the hoop has larger rotational inertia than the disk, so angular acceleration decreases; or an energy explanation that more energy goes into rotation and less into translation. A derivation such as $a=mgR^2/(I+mR^2)$, or an equivalent expression, with an explanation that the hoop has larger rotational inertia is also acceptable.

subject: AP Physics C: Mechanics
source: AP Physics C: Mechanics 2014 Practice Exam
text:
Experiment 1: A block of mass $1.5\,\mathrm{kg}$ is placed on a long board. You are to design an experiment to determine the coefficient of static friction between the block and the board.

(a)

  i. From the following list of available equipment, check those additional items you would use for the purpose of determining the coefficient of static friction.

- [ ] Ruler
- [ ] Spring scale
- [ ] String
- [ ] Meterstick
- [ ] Pulley
- [ ] Protractor
- [ ] Photogate
- [ ] Stopwatch
- [ ] Mass hanger
- [ ] Clamps and supports
- [ ] Objects of various known masses

  ii. Sketch a diagram of your experimental setup and label the pieces of equipment that would be used.

  iii. Outline the experimental procedure you would use, including a list of quantities you would measure. For each quantity, identify the equipment you would use to make the measurement.

(b) Explain how to use the measurements described in part (a) to calculate the coefficient of static friction. Include a free-body diagram in your explanation that shows all forces, not components, acting on the block while the measurements are being made.

Experiment 2: In a second experiment, the coefficient of kinetic friction between the block and the board is determined to be $0.10$. The board is now inclined at an angle of $25^\circ$ above the horizontal. The block is released from rest at the top of the incline and slides $2.0\,\mathrm{m}$ down the incline.

(c) Calculate the work done by kinetic friction as the block slides down the incline.

(d) The mass of the block is now increased without changing the coefficient of kinetic friction, and experiment 2 is repeated. How does each of the following change?

  i. The magnitude of the frictional force

- [ ] Increases
- [ ] Decreases
- [ ] Remains the same

  ii. The magnitude of the velocity of the block as it reaches the bottom of the incline

- [ ] Increases
- [ ] Decreases
- [ ] Remains the same

  iii. The kinetic energy of the block at the bottom of the incline

- [ ] Increases
- [ ] Decreases
- [ ] Remains the same

points.description: Part (a)(i): Award 1 point for selecting equipment consistent with the diagram drawn in part (a)(ii).
Part (a)(ii): Award 1 point for labeling a diagram consistent with the procedure described in part (a)(iii).

Part (a)(iii): Award 1 point for listing specific quantities to be measured that can be used to determine the coefficient of static friction, and 1 point for indicating which equipment will be used to take those measurements. Acceptable examples include pulling the block with a spring scale until it starts to move and measuring the maximum applied force, or raising the board and measuring the angle at which the block begins to slide.

Part (b): The experiment must attempt to measure the coefficient of static friction $\mu_s$, not the coefficient of kinetic friction. For a horizontal-board method, award 1 point for vectors indicating the normal force up and the force of gravity down, 1 point for a vector indicating the horizontal applied force, 1 point for a vector indicating static friction opposite the applied force, 1 point for setting the net force equal to zero, and 1 point for a correct expression for the coefficient of static friction, such as $\mu_s=F_{\max}/(mg)$. Deduct 1 point if there are extraneous vectors or vectors not starting on and pointing away from the block. For an incline method, award analogous points for gravity downward, the normal force perpendicular to the incline, static friction up the incline, the zero-net-force condition, and the result $\mu_s=\tan\theta$.

Part (c): Award 1 point for a correct equation for the work done by kinetic friction, such as $W=\vec F\cdot \vec d=-f_kd$ or $W=-\mu_k mg\cos\theta\,d$; 1 point for correctly substituting for the force of kinetic friction; and 1 point for the correct answer $W=-2.7\,\mathrm{J}$. Answers without the negative sign lose only the answer point.
Part (d)(i): Award 1 point for selecting "Increases" for the magnitude of the frictional force.
Part (d)(ii): Award 1 point for selecting "Remains the same" for the magnitude of the velocity of the block as it reaches the bottom of the incline.
Part (d)(iii): Award 1 point for selecting "Increases" for the kinetic energy of the block at the bottom of the incline.

subject: AP Physics C: Mechanics
source: AP Physics C: Mechanics 2014 Practice Exam
text:
A satellite of mass $m$ is in a stable circular orbit around Earth at a distance $R_1$ from the center of Earth.

The mass of Earth is $M_E$.

(a) Derive an expression for the following in terms of $m$, $R_1$, $M_E$, and fundamental constants, as appropriate.

  i. The orbital speed $v_1$ of the satellite

  ii. The total energy of the satellite in this orbit, assuming gravitational potential energy to be zero at an infinite distance from the center of Earth

The satellite's booster rockets fire and lift the satellite to a higher circular orbit of radius $2R_1$. The satellite follows the path shown in the diagram below, moving a total distance $S$ during the orbital change. The component of the rockets' force parallel to the path is given by the equation $F=F_0\left(1-\frac{x}{S}\right)$, where $x$ is the variable distance traveled along the path at any moment.

(b) Derive an expression for the total work done on the satellite by the force $F$ in terms of $F_0$ and $S$.

(c) If the total distance $S$ is equal to $3R_1$, derive an expression for $F_0$ in terms of $M_E$, $R_1$, $m$, and fundamental constants, as appropriate.

points.description: Part (a)(i): Set the gravitational force equal to the centripetal force, $F_g=F_C$. Award 1 point for a correct expression for the gravitational force, 1 point for a correct expression for the centripetal force, and 1 point for the correct answer $v_1=\sqrt{GM_E/R_1}$.

Part (a)(ii): Award 1 point for expressing the total energy as the sum of gravitational potential energy and kinetic energy, $E=U_g+K$; 1 point for substituting a correct expression for the kinetic energy of the satellite, such as $E=-GM_E m/R_1+(1/2)m v_1^2$; and 1 point for the correct answer $E=-GM_E m/(2R_1)$.

Part (b): Award 1 point for a correct equation for the work done expressed as the integral of the force, $W=\int \vec F\cdot d\vec s$; 1 point for substituting the given expression for force, $W=\int_0^S F_0\left(1-x/S\right)\,dx$; 1 point for integrating with the correct limits, $W=F_0[x-x^2/(2S)]_0^S$; and 1 point for the correct answer $W=F_0S/2$.

Part (c): Award 1 point for correctly relating work and change in energy, $W=\Delta E$; 1 point for substituting the correct initial radius into the expression for potential energy; 1 point for substituting the correct final radius into the expression for potential energy, so $W=\Delta E=-GM_E m/(4R_1)-[-GM_E m/(2R_1)]$; 1 point for a correct substitution of the answer from part (b), $F_0S/2=-GM_E m/(4R_1)+GM_E m/(2R_1)$ and $F_0(3R_1)/2=GM_E m/(4R_1)$; and 1 point for an answer consistent with part (b) and the change in energy, $F_0=GM_E m/(6R_1^2)$.

subject: AP Physics C: Mechanics
source: AP Physics C: Mechanics 2014 Practice Exam
text:
A uniform disk of mass $M$ and radius $R$ is suspended vertically from a pivot on its edge so that it is free to swing without friction, as shown in Figure 1 above. The disk is raised to an initial angular displacement $\theta_0$ with respect to the vertical, as shown in Figure 2 above, and released from rest. The rotational inertia of the disk about its center of mass is $I=MR^2/2$. Express all algebraic answers in terms of the given quantities and fundamental constants, as appropriate.

(a) Derive an expression for the rotational inertia of the disk about the pivot.

(b) Derive an expression for the maximum angular velocity of the disk during its swing.

(c) Using Newton's second law in either translational or rotational form, as appropriate, write a differential equation that could be used to determine the angular displacement of the disk $\theta$ as a function of time $t$. Do not solve the equation.

(d) Derive an expression for the period for small-amplitude oscillations of the disk.

(e) Suppose the disk is replaced with another uniform disk with twice the radius, and the pivot point is again on the edge of the disk. How will the period of small-amplitude oscillations be affected by the new disk?

- [ ] It will increase.
- [ ] It will decrease.
- [ ] It will remain the same.
- [ ] There is not enough information to determine the period.

Justify your answer.

points.description: Part (a): Award 1 point for a correct expression of the parallel-axis theorem, $I=I_{CM}+Md^2$, and 1 point for a correct answer after substituting into the expression: $I=\frac{1}{2}MR^2+MR^2=\frac{3}{2}MR^2$.

Part (b): Award 1 point for a correct expression of conservation of energy for the disk, $K_1+U_1=K_2+U_2$; 1 point for correctly setting the initial gravitational potential energy equal to the rotational kinetic energy at the bottom of the swing, $Mgh_1=\frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$; 1 point for substituting a correct expression for the initial height, giving $Mg(R-R\cos\theta_0)=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{3}{2}MR^2\right)\omega^2$; and 1 point for an answer consistent with part (a), $\omega=\sqrt{\frac{4g}{3R}(1-\cos\theta_0)}$.

Part (c): Award 1 point for using a correct expression of Newton's second law for rotational motion, $\tau=I\alpha=\frac{3}{2}MR^2\alpha$; 1 point for substituting a correct expression for torque into the equation, $-MgR\sin\theta=\frac{3}{2}MR^2\alpha$; and 1 point for expressing the equation as a differential equation. Any appropriate manipulation is acceptable, including a subsequent small-angle approximation. One acceptable form is $-g\sin\theta=\frac{3}{2}R\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2}$.

Part (d): Award 1 point for using a correct expression for simple harmonic motion, such as $a=-\omega^2x$ or $\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2}=-\omega^2\theta$; 1 point for substituting the small-angle approximation, $\sin\theta\approx\theta$, into the answer from part (c), giving $-g\theta=\frac{3}{2}R\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2}$ and $-\frac{2g}{3R}\theta=\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2}$; 1 point for relating the correct angular frequency to the period, $\omega=\sqrt{2g/(3R)}=2\pi/T$; and 1 point for the correct answer $T=2\pi\sqrt{3R/(2g)}$.

Part (e): Award 1 point for selecting "It will increase" and 1 point for a correct justification. For example, $T\propto\sqrt{R}$, so doubling the radius increases the period by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$.

content_blocks.text: A uniform disk of mass $M$ and radius $R$ is suspended vertically from a pivot on its edge so that it is free to swing without friction, as shown in Figure 1 above. The disk is raised to an initial angular displacement $\theta_0$ with respect to the vertical, as shown in Figure 2 above, and released from rest. The rotational inertia of the disk about its center of mass is $I=MR^2/2$. Express all algebraic answers in terms of the given quantities and fundamental constants, as appropriate.

content_blocks.label: (a)
content_blocks.text: Derive an expression for the rotational inertia of the disk about the pivot.

content_blocks.label: (b)
content_blocks.text: Derive an expression for the maximum angular velocity of the disk during its swing.

content_blocks.label: (c)
content_blocks.text: Using Newton's second law in either translational or rotational form, as appropriate, write a differential equation that could be used to determine the angular displacement of the disk $\theta$ as a function of time $t$. Do not solve the equation.

content_blocks.label: (d)
content_blocks.text: Derive an expression for the period for small-amplitude oscillations of the disk.

content_blocks.label: (e)
content_blocks.text: Suppose the disk is replaced with another uniform disk with twice the radius, and the pivot point is again on the edge of the disk. How will the period of small-amplitude oscillations be affected by the new disk? - [ ] It will increase. - [ ] It will decrease. - [ ] It will remain the same. - [ ] There is not enough information to determine the period. Justify your answer.

subject: AP Physics C: Mechanics
source: AP Physics C: Mechanics 2015 Practice Exam
text:
A new sports car is undergoing acceleration tests to determine its specifications. The following data on speed $v$ versus time $t$ are recorded for the car as it accelerates from rest along a straight track.

| $t$ (s) | 0 | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| $v$ (m/s) | 0 | 14 | 23 | 30 | 35 | 39 |

(a) On the axes below, plot $v$ as a function of $t$ and sketch a curve that best represents the data.

(b) Answer each of the following for the time period $t=0\,\mathrm{s}$ to $t=10\,\mathrm{s}$.

  i. Does the speed of the car increase, decrease, or stay the same?

- [ ] Increase
- [ ] Decrease
- [ ] Stay the same

Justify your answer.

  ii. Does the acceleration of the car increase, decrease, or stay the same?

- [ ] Increase
- [ ] Decrease
- [ ] Stay the same

Justify your answer.

(c) Explain how you would use your graph in part (a) to find the distance traveled by the car between $t=2\,\mathrm{s}$ and $t=8\,\mathrm{s}$.

The equation for the speed $v$ of the car as a function of time $t$ found from the graph is

$$v(t)=-0.3t^2+7t,$$

where $v$ is in $\mathrm{m/s}$ and $t$ is in seconds.

(d) Derive an expression for the acceleration of the car $a(t)$ as a function of time $t$.

(e) Calculate the position of the car as a function of time, $x(t)$, assuming that the car starts from rest at the origin of a coordinate system.

(f) Calculate the distance traveled by the car between $t=2\,\mathrm{s}$ and $t=8\,\mathrm{s}$.

points.description: Part (a): Award 1 point for accurately plotting at least five points, 1 point for a smooth curve with positive slope, and 1 point for a concave-down curve starting at the origin.
Part (b)(i): Award 1 point for choosing "Increase" with a correct justification, such as the speed increasing as the magnitude of velocity increases for each data point.
Part (b)(ii): Award 1 point for choosing "Decrease" with a correct justification, such as the slope of the velocity-time graph decreasing with time, so acceleration decreases.

Part (c): Award 1 point for mentioning the area under the velocity-time curve and 1 point for specifically describing the area between $t=2\,\mathrm{s}$ and $t=8\,\mathrm{s}$. For example, approximate the area under the curve over that interval with a trapezoid or other reasonable area estimate.
Part (d): Award 1 point for indicating that acceleration is the derivative of velocity, $a(t)=dv/dt$. Award 1 point for correctly evaluating the derivative: $a(t)=d(-0.3t^2+7t)/dt=-0.6t+7$.

Part (e): Award 1 point for indicating that displacement is the integral of velocity, $\Delta x=\int_0^t v(t)\,dt$. Award 1 point for attempting to integrate with appropriate limits or a constant of integration. Award 1 point for the correct answer $x(t)=-0.10t^3+3.5t^2$.

Part (f): Award 1 point for recognizing that the distance is the change in position, $\Delta x=x(8)-x(2)$. Award 1 point for using the expression from part (e) to calculate $x(2)=13.2\,\mathrm{m}$ and $x(8)=172.8\,\mathrm{m}$. Award 1 point for the correct answer with units, $\Delta x=159.6\,\mathrm{m}$. An equivalent definite integral, $\int_2^8(-0.3t^2+7t)\,dt$, earns the same points.

text:
The model of a roller coaster shown above consists of a ramp and a circular loop. A small block of mass $M$ starts from height $H$ with speed $v=0$ and slides without friction down the ramp and around the inside of the loop of radius $H/2$. Position $P$ is a point on the loop at an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal, as shown above. Express all algebraic answers in terms of $M$, $H$, $\theta$, and fundamental constants, as appropriate.

(a) Derive an expression for the speed of the block at position $P$.

(b) On the following diagram, draw and label vectors to represent the forces, not components, on the block when it is at position $P$. Each force must be represented by a distinct arrow starting on, and pointing away from, the dot at position $P$.

(c) Derive an expression for the magnitude of the force exerted by the loop on the block when it is at position $P$. If you need to draw anything other than what you have shown in part (b) to assist in your solution, use the space below. Do not add anything to the figure in part (b).

(d) Calculate the angle $\theta_L$, in degrees, at which the block leaves the loop.

(e) The small block is replaced with a small sphere that also starts at height $H$. Now there is enough friction to allow the sphere to roll down the ramp without slipping. Identify how the new angle $\theta_n$ at which the sphere leaves the loop compares with the angle $\theta_L$ calculated in part (d).

- [ ] $\theta_n<\theta_L$
- [ ] $\theta_n=\theta_L$
- [ ] $\theta_n>\theta_L$

Explain your reasoning.
unit_name: Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems

topics.name: Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems

points.description: Part (a): Award 1 point for a correct application of conservation of energy, such as $K_i+U_i=K_f+U_f$ or $MgH=\frac12Mv_P^2+Mgy_P$. Award 1 point for relating the height at $P$ to $H$ and $\theta$, $y_P=H/2+(H/2)\sin\theta$. Award 1 point for a correct final expression, $v_P=\sqrt{gH(1-\sin\theta)}$.

Part (b): Award 1 point for correctly drawing and labeling the weight vector of the block and 1 point for correctly drawing and labeling the normal force on the block. Deduct one earned point, up to 2 points, for each extraneous force.

Part (c): Award 1 point for applying Newton's second law with more than one force, 1 point for setting the radial force sum equal to $Mv^2/R$, 1 point for using the correct radial component of weight, $Mg\sin\theta$, 1 point for substituting the speed from part (a) and the radius $H/2$, and 1 point for a final expression consistent with part (a). The expected result is $F_N=Mg(2-3\sin\theta)$.

Part (d): Award 1 point for setting the normal force equal to zero, explicitly or implicitly: $0=Mg(2-3\sin\theta)$, so $3\sin\theta=2$. Award 1 point for the angle consistent with part (c), $\theta_L=\sin^{-1}(2/3)=41.8^\circ$.

Part (e): Award 1 point for correctly selecting $\theta_n<\theta_L$. Award 1 point for indicating that the sphere will be moving slower, or has less translational kinetic energy, when it loses contact. Award 1 point for indicating that some of the initial potential energy goes into rotational kinetic energy. Therefore the sphere loses contact at a lower height and smaller angle.

subject: AP Physics C: Mechanics
source: AP Physics C: Mechanics 2015 Practice Exam
text:
A uniform, rigid, thin board that is $2.4\,\mathrm{m}$ long and weighs $200\,\mathrm{N}$ is attached to a post by a pivot at point $P$ and hangs over the edge of a building, as shown above. A crate weighing $400\,\mathrm{N}$ is attached to the right end of the board. The left end of the board, $0.80\,\mathrm{m}$ from the pivot, is attached to a light vertical wire anchored at the other end to keep the board from rotating.

(a) On the diagram below, draw and label vectors to represent all the forces, not components, acting on the board. Show each force vector originating at its point of application.

(b) Calculate the magnitudes of the forces exerted on the board by the post and by the wire. If you need to draw anything other than what you have shown in part (a) to assist in your solution, use the space below. Do not add anything to the figure in part (a).

(c) The rotational inertia of the board alone about its center is $\frac{1}{12}ML^2$, where $M$ is the mass of the board and $L$ is its length. Calculate the rotational inertia of the combined board-crate system about point $P$.

(d) Suppose that the wire breaks and the board begins to pivot about point $P$. Calculate each of the following.

  i. The magnitude of the initial angular acceleration of the board-crate system

  ii. The magnitude of the initial linear acceleration of the left end of the board

points.description: Part (a): Award 1 point for correctly drawing and labeling the three downward forces: tension, weight of the board, and weight of the crate. Award 1 point for correctly drawing and labeling the upward force exerted on the board by the post. Award 1 point for drawing all forces at their respective points of action. Deduct one earned point, up to 2 points, for each extraneous force.

Part (b): Award 1 point for summing torques about any labeled point and setting the sum equal to zero. Using the left end as pivot, $(200\,\mathrm{N})(1.2\,\mathrm{m})+(400\,\mathrm{N})(2.4\,\mathrm{m})=F_{post}(0.8\,\mathrm{m})$. Award 1 point for substituting correct lever arms for all torques. Award 1 point for correctly calculating either the wire tension or the post force, such as $F_{post}=1500\,\mathrm{N}$. Award 1 point for a correct method for the remaining force and a consistent answer, for example $F_T=1500\,\mathrm{N}-200\,\mathrm{N}-400\,\mathrm{N}=900\,\mathrm{N}$.

Part (c): Award 1 point for correctly calculating the rotational inertia of the board about $P$, $I_{board,P}=Md^2+I_{CM}=Md^2+\frac{1}{12}ML^2$, giving about $13.1\,\mathrm{kg\cdot m^2}$, or $12.8\,\mathrm{kg\cdot m^2}$ if $g=10\,\mathrm{m/s^2}$ is used. Award 1 point for correctly calculating the crate's rotational inertia about $P$, about $104.5\,\mathrm{kg\cdot m^2}$, or $102.4\,\mathrm{kg\cdot m^2}$ if $g=10\,\mathrm{m/s^2}$ is used. Award 1 point for recognizing that the total rotational inertia about $P$ is the sum, about $117.6\,\mathrm{kg\cdot m^2}$, or $115.2\,\mathrm{kg\cdot m^2}$ if $g=10\,\mathrm{m/s^2}$ is used.

Part (d)(i): Award 1 point for summing torques about $P$, setting the sum equal to $I\alpha$, and substituting appropriate values, such as $(200\,\mathrm{N})(0.4\,\mathrm{m})+(400\,\mathrm{N})(1.6\,\mathrm{m})=I\alpha$. Award 1 point for an angular acceleration consistent with part (c), about $6.13\,\mathrm{rad/s^2}$, or $6.25\,\mathrm{rad/s^2}$ if $g=10\,\mathrm{m/s^2}$ is used.

Part (d)(ii): Award 1 point for a correct relationship between angular and linear acceleration, $a=r\alpha$. Award 1 point for an answer consistent with part (d)(i), $a=(0.80\,\mathrm{m})(6.13\,\mathrm{rad/s^2})=4.9\,\mathrm{m/s^2}$, or $5.0\,\mathrm{m/s^2}$ if $g=10\,\mathrm{m/s^2}$ is used.
Units: Award 1 point for correct units on all numerical answers.

subject: AP Physics C: Mechanics
source: AP Physics C: Mechanics 2016 Practice Exam
text:
A block of mass $m_B$ is being pulled up a ramp by a hanging object of mass $m_H$, as shown in the figure above. The blocks are attached to each other by a light string that passes over a pulley of negligible mass and friction. Both the block and the hanging object are moving at constant speed, and the angle of the ramp with the horizontal is $\theta$. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the block and the ramp is $\mu_k$.

(a) On the dots below, which represent the block and the hanging object, draw and label the forces, not components, that act on the block and the hanging object. Each force must be represented by a distinct arrow starting on, and pointing away from, the appropriate dot.

(b) Derive an expression for the mass $m_H$ of the hanging object. Express your answer in terms of $m_B$, $\theta$, $\mu_k$, and physical constants, as appropriate. If you need to draw anything other than what you have shown in part (a) to assist in your solution, use the space below. Do not add anything to the figure in part (a).

It is determined that $m_B=2.0\,\mathrm{kg}$, $\theta=30^\circ$, and $\mu_k=0.10$. While the block is moving up the ramp and the hanging object is moving downward, the string is suddenly cut.

(c)

  i. Calculate the magnitude of the acceleration of the block immediately after the string is cut.

  ii. What is the direction of the acceleration of the block immediately after the string is cut?

- [ ] Up the ramp
- [ ] Down the ramp
- [ ] No direction, because the acceleration is zero

  iii. Determine the magnitude of the acceleration of the hanging object immediately after the string is cut.

(d) Assume the block is moving up the ramp at $2.0\,\mathrm{m/s}$ when the string is cut. Calculate how far the block travels up the ramp from the time the string is cut until the block stops, assuming there is sufficient distance on the ramp.

points.description: Part (a): Award 1 point for correctly drawing and labeling the force of tension and the force of friction for the block; 1 point for correctly drawing and labeling the normal force vector for the block; 1 point for correctly drawing and labeling the weight vector for the block; and 1 point for correctly drawing and labeling both vertical vectors for the hanging object. A maximum of 3 points can be earned if there are any extraneous vectors.

Part (b): Award 1 point for a correct Newton's second law equation for the two-block system, for example $m_Hg-F_T=m_Ha=0$ and $F_T-m_Bg\sin\theta-f=m_Ba=0$, or $m_Hg-m_Bg\sin\theta-f=(m_H+m_B)a=0$. Award 1 point for setting the acceleration equal to zero, either implicitly or explicitly, giving $m_Hg-m_Bg\sin\theta-\mu_km_Bg\cos\theta=0$. Award 1 point for the correct result $m_H=m_B(\sin\theta+\mu_k\cos\theta)$.

Part (c)(i): Award 1 point for correctly applying Newton's second law to the block after the string is cut, $-m_Bg\sin\theta-\mu_km_Bg\cos\theta=m_Ba$, or $-g(\sin\theta+\mu_k\cos\theta)=a$. Award 1 point for correctly substituting $a=-(9.8\,\mathrm{m/s^2})((0.10)\cos30^\circ+\sin30^\circ)$. Award 1 point for the correct magnitude $5.75\,\mathrm{m/s^2}$; $5.87\,\mathrm{m/s^2}$ is acceptable if $g=10\,\mathrm{m/s^2}$ is used.
Part (c)(ii): Award 1 point for selecting "Down the ramp."
Part (c)(iii): Award 1 point for the correct answer $a=g=9.81\,\mathrm{m/s^2}$; $10\,\mathrm{m/s^2}$ is also acceptable. No work needs to be shown for this part.

Part (d): Award 1 point for using a correct kinematics equation, such as $v_2^2=v_1^2+2ad$. Award 1 point for setting the final velocity equal to zero and solving $0=v_1^2+2ad$, so $d=-v_1^2/(2a)=-(2.0\,\mathrm{m/s})^2/((2)(-5.75\,\mathrm{m/s^2}))$. Award 1 point for the correct answer $d=0.348\,\mathrm{m}$; $0.341\,\mathrm{m}$ is acceptable if $g=10\,\mathrm{m/s^2}$ is used. An alternate energy solution may earn the same points by using $KE_i+W_f=PE_f$, converting $h$ to $d$ with $h=d\sin\theta$, and obtaining the same distance.

subject: AP Physics C: Mechanics
source: AP Physics C: Mechanics 2016 Practice Exam
text:
An experiment is performed on a frictionless surface consisting of a horizontal section and a ramp inclined at a $20^\circ$ angle. Block A of mass $3.0\,\mathrm{kg}$ rests on the horizontal section against but not attached to an ideal spring at its equilibrium length, as shown in the figure above. The spring has a spring constant $k=700\,\mathrm{N/m}$. Block A is pushed to the left, compressing the spring $0.15\,\mathrm{m}$, and then released.

(a) Calculate the speed of block A when it leaves the spring.

(b) Calculate the maximum distance $d$ block A travels up the ramp.

(c) On the axis below, sketch a graph of the speed $v$ of block A as a function of time $t$ from $t=0$ to $t=t_3$. Block A is released at time $t=0$, loses contact with the spring at time $t=t_1$, transitions to the ramp at time $t=t_2$, and reaches its maximum height at time $t=t_3$.

In a second experiment, block B, of mass $0.50\,\mathrm{kg}$, is placed on the ramp a distance of $d$ from the bottom of the ramp and held in place. Block A is released from the spring as in the first experiment. Block B is released from rest so that it collides with block A as block A travels up the ramp. The two blocks collide at a distance of $d/2$ up the ramp. The blocks stick together after the collision.

(d)

  i. Calculate the speed of the two blocks immediately after the collision.

  ii. In what direction, if any, are the two blocks moving immediately after the collision?

- [ ] Up the ramp
- [ ] Down the ramp
- [ ] Undefined, because the blocks momentarily come to rest after the collision

(e)

  i. Calculate the magnitude of the impulse exerted on block A by block B.

  ii. In what direction, if any, is the impulse exerted on block A by block B?

- [ ] Up the ramp
- [ ] Down the ramp
- [ ] Undefined, because the impulse is zero

points.description: Part (a): Award 1 point for correctly using conservation of energy to determine the speed of block A, $U_{s1}=K_2$, $\frac12kx^2=\frac12mv^2$, so $v=\sqrt{k/m}\,x=\sqrt{(700\,\mathrm{N/m})/(3.0\,\mathrm{kg})}(0.15\,\mathrm{m})$. Award 1 point for the correct answer $v=2.29\,\mathrm{m/s}$.

Part (b): Award 1 point for correctly using conservation of energy to determine the distance block A travels up the ramp: $K_1=U_{g2}$, $\frac12mv^2=mgh=mgd\sin\theta$. Thus $d=mv^2/(2mg\sin\theta)=(3.0\,\mathrm{kg})(2.29\,\mathrm{m/s})^2/((2)(3.0\,\mathrm{kg})(9.8\,\mathrm{m/s^2})\sin20^\circ)$. Award 1 point for $d=0.783\,\mathrm{m}$; $0.767\,\mathrm{m}$ is acceptable if $g=10\,\mathrm{m/s^2}$ is used. An equivalent spring-energy solution using $\frac12kx^2=mgd\sin\theta$ is acceptable.

Part (c): Award 1 point for a concave-down curve that starts at the origin at $t=0$ and becomes horizontal at $t=t_1$; 1 point for a horizontal line from $t=t_1$ to $t=t_2$ that is continuous with the first section; and 1 point for a straight line with negative slope from $t=t_2$ to the horizontal axis at $t=t_3$ that is continuous with the second section.

Part (d)(i): Award up to 5 points. Award 1 point for correctly using conservation of energy to determine block A's speed at the collision, $K_1=U_{g2}+K_2$, $\frac12mv_1^2=mg(d/2)\sin\theta+\frac12mv_2^2$. Award 1 point for correct substitution, $v_1^2=gd\sin\theta+v_2^2$, giving $v_A=1.62\,\mathrm{m/s}$; $1.60\,\mathrm{m/s}$ is acceptable if $g=10\,\mathrm{m/s^2}$ is used. Award 1 point for correctly using energy to determine block B's speed, $mg(d/2)\sin\theta=\frac12mv^2$, giving $v_B=1.62\,\mathrm{m/s}$; $1.64\,\mathrm{m/s}$ is acceptable if $g=10\,\mathrm{m/s^2}$ is used. Award 1 point for correctly using conservation of momentum, $m_Av_A+m_Bv_B=(m_A+m_B)v_f$, with block B's velocity opposite block A's. Award 1 point for the correct result $v_f=1.16\,\mathrm{m/s}$; $1.14\,\mathrm{m/s}$ is acceptable if $g=10\,\mathrm{m/s^2}$ is used. Equivalent kinematics for finding $v_A$ and $v_B$ earns the corresponding points.
Part (d)(ii): Award 1 point for selecting "Up the ramp."

Part (e)(i): Award 1 point for correctly using impulse, $J=m(v_2-v_1)$, with a result consistent with part (d). For example, $J=(3.0\,\mathrm{kg})(1.16\,\mathrm{m/s}-1.62\,\mathrm{m/s})=-1.38\,\mathrm{N\cdot s}$. The negative sign is not required because the question asks for magnitude.
Part (e)(ii): Award 1 point for selecting "Down the ramp."

source: AP Physics C: Mechanics 2016 Practice Exam
text:
A uniform rod of mass $M$ and length $L$ is pivoted at a point a distance $L/3$ from the top end, as shown above. The rod is pulled back so that it makes a small angle with the vertical and is then released.

(a) Using integral calculus, show that the rotational inertia for the rod around its pivot is $ML^2/9$.

(b)

  i. Using Newton's second law in rotational form, write but do not solve a differential equation in terms of $M$, $L$, and physical constants, as appropriate, that can be used to determine the angular displacement $\theta$ as a function of time $t$.

  ii. Using the differential equation from part (b)(i), show that the period of oscillation for the rod is given by

$$T=2\pi\sqrt{\frac{2L}{3g}}.$$

An experiment is performed with thin, uniform metal rods of different lengths, each pivoted around a point one third the length of the rod from the top end and used as a physical pendulum, as shown in the figure on the previous page. Each rod is pulled back so that it makes a small angle with the vertical and is then released, and the period of oscillation is measured. The data are recorded in the table below.

| Length (m) | 0.5 | 1.0 | 1.5 | 2.0 | 2.5 |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Period (s) | 1.2 | 1.6 | 2.0 | 2.3 | 2.6 |

(c) Indicate below which quantities should be graphed to yield a straight line whose slope could be used to calculate a numerical value for the acceleration due to gravity $g$.

Horizontal axis:

Vertical axis:

Use the remaining rows in the table above, as needed, to record any quantities that you indicated that are not given.

(d) Plot the straight-line data points on the graph below. Clearly scale and label all axes, including units if appropriate. Draw a straight line that best represents the data.

(e) Using your straight line, determine an experimental value for $g$.

points.description: Part (a): Award 1 point for using integral calculus to attempt to calculate the rotational inertia of the rod, such as $I=\int r^2\,dm$ with $\lambda=M/L$ and $dm=\lambda\,dr$, so $I=\int \lambda r^2\,dr$. Award 1 point for correctly integrating the expression, $I=\lambda[r^3/3]$. Award 1 point for using correct limits or a correct constant of integration, for example from $r=-L/3$ to $r=2L/3$, giving $I=ML^2/9$. An alternate solution may first find $I_{CM}=\frac{1}{12}ML^2$ by integration about the center and then use the parallel-axis theorem, $I=I_{CM}+Mh^2=\frac{1}{12}ML^2+M(L/6)^2=ML^2/9$.

Part (b)(i): Award 1 point for using a correct expression of Newton's second law for rotation of the rod, $\tau=F\ell_\perp=I\alpha$, such as $-Mg(L/6)\sin\theta=(ML^2/9)\alpha$. Award 1 point for expressing the equation as a differential equation, for example $-(3g/(2L))\sin\theta=d^2\theta/dt^2$.

Part (b)(ii): Award 1 point for using the small-angle approximation, $\sin\theta\approx\theta$, giving $-(3g/(2L))\theta=d^2\theta/dt^2$. Award 1 point for comparing this equation to rotational simple harmonic motion or solving the differential equation: $-(3g/(2L))\theta=d^2\theta/dt^2=\alpha=-\omega^2\theta$, so $\omega^2=(2\pi/T)^2=3g/(2L)$ and $T=2\pi\sqrt{2L/(3g)}$.

Part (c): Award 1 point for selecting appropriate variables that create a straight-line relationship. An acceptable example is horizontal axis: length; vertical axis: period squared. Reversing the axes is acceptable, and graphing square root of length versus period is also acceptable.

Part (d): Award 1 point for labeling both axes with appropriate variables and units; 1 point for a correct scale that uses more than half the grid; 1 point for correctly plotting data indicated in part (c); and 1 point for drawing a straight line consistent with the given data.

Part (e): Award 1 point for correctly calculating slope using the best-fit straight line and not only data points, for example $\mathrm{slope}=(y_2-y_1)/(x_2-x_1)=(6.0-2.0)/(2.20-0.80)=2.86\,\mathrm{s^2/m}$. Linear regression gives about $2.67\,\mathrm{s^2/m}$. Award 1 point for correctly relating $g$ to the slope using $T=2\pi\sqrt{2L/(3g)}$, so $T^2=(4\pi^2)(2L)/(3g)$ and $\mathrm{slope}=(4\pi^2)(2)/(3g)$. Award 1 point for a correct value, for example $g=8\pi^2/(3\times\mathrm{slope})=9.20\,\mathrm{m/s^2}$; linear regression gives about $9.86\,\mathrm{m/s^2}$.

subject: AP Physics C: Mechanics
source: AP Physics C: Mechanics 2017 Practice Exam
text:
A spring of negligible mass and spring constant $50\,\mathrm{N/m}$ is hung vertically, as shown above in the figure on the left. A block of mass $1.0\,\mathrm{kg}$ is attached to the spring and slowly lowered a distance $x_0$ until it hangs at rest at the equilibrium position, as shown above in the figure on the right.

(a) On the dot below that represents the block, draw and label the forces, not components, that act on the block when it is at the equilibrium position shown. Each force must be represented by a distinct arrow starting on, and pointing away from, the dot.

(b) Calculate the distance $x_0$ that the spring is stretched from its original length to the equilibrium position.

While the $1.0\,\mathrm{kg}$ block is in equilibrium, a $0.20\,\mathrm{kg}$ ball of clay is launched with speed $v_0$ vertically from the ground $1.0\,\mathrm{m}$ below the hanging block. The clay has speed $v_1=5.0\,\mathrm{m/s}$ when it collides with the block.

(c) Calculate the speed $v_0$.

Upon impact, the clay sticks to the $1.0\,\mathrm{kg}$ block.

(d)

  i. Calculate the speed of the block immediately after the collision.

  ii. Calculate the kinetic energy lost in the collision.

  iii. Determine the period of oscillation for the block-clay-spring system.

(e) When the oscillating block-clay-spring system reaches its maximum speed after the collision, will the spring be stretched from its original length by a distance greater than, less than, or equal to $x_0$?

- [ ] Greater than
- [ ] Less than
- [ ] Equal to

Justify your answer.

(f) The collision is repeated using a rubber ball with the same mass as the ball of clay. The rubber ball is launched vertically with the same speed $v_0$. The collision between the rubber ball and the block is elastic. Will the speed of the block immediately after colliding with the rubber ball be greater than, less than, or equal to the speed of the block you calculated in part (d)(i)?

- [ ] Greater than
- [ ] Less than
- [ ] Equal to

Justify your answer.

points.description: Part (a): Award 1 point for correctly drawing and labeling the weight of the block and 1 point for correctly drawing and labeling the spring force. A maximum of 1 point can be earned if there are extraneous vectors.

Part (b): Award 1 point for correctly substituting spring and gravitational forces into Newton's second law, $F_s=mg$ and $kx_0=mg$. Award 1 point for the correct answer $x_0=mg/k=(1.0\,\mathrm{kg})(9.8\,\mathrm{m/s^2})/(50\,\mathrm{N/m})=0.20\,\mathrm{m}$.

Part (c): Award 1 point for using appropriate kinematics or energy to determine the initial speed of the clay, such as $v_1^2=v_0^2+2a\Delta y$ or $v_0^2=v_1^2+2gh$. Award 1 point for the correct answer with units, $v_0=6.68\,\mathrm{m/s}$.

Part (d)(i): Award 1 point for using conservation of momentum to determine the speed just after the collision, $m_cv_1=(m_c+m_b)v_f$. Award 1 point for the correct answer with units, $v_f=(0.20\,\mathrm{kg})(5.0\,\mathrm{m/s})/(0.20\,\mathrm{kg}+1.0\,\mathrm{kg})=0.83\,\mathrm{m/s}$.

Part (d)(ii): Award 1 point for substituting the correct mass and initial speed, or the same mass and speed as in part (d)(i), into kinetic energy, $K_i=\frac12(0.20\,\mathrm{kg})(5.0\,\mathrm{m/s})^2=2.5\,\mathrm{J}$. Award 1 point for calculating final kinetic energy with the combined mass and speed from part (d)(i), $K_f=\frac12(1.20\,\mathrm{kg})(0.83\,\mathrm{m/s})^2=0.41\,\mathrm{J}$, and subtracting to get about $2.1\,\mathrm{J}$ lost. A maximum of 1 point can be earned if other energy forms are included in this collision calculation.
Part (d)(iii): Award 1 point for using the combined mass in the period equation for a spring, $T=2\pi\sqrt{(m_b+m_c)/k}=2\pi\sqrt{(1.0+0.20)/(50)}=0.97\,\mathrm{s}$.

Part (e): Award 1 point for any correct equilibrium statement even if the wrong selection is made. Award 1 point for selecting "Greater than" and indicating that the greater mass stretches the spring more at equilibrium. Maximum speed occurs at the new equilibrium position, so the spring is longer than $x_0$ when the block-clay system has maximum speed.

Part (f): Award 1 point for correctly selecting "Greater than" and attempting to justify the choice. Award 1 point for a correct justification, such as kinetic energy being conserved in an elastic collision, causing the target block to leave with greater speed than in the perfectly inelastic clay collision. If the wrong selection is made, the justification cannot earn credit.

subject: AP Physics C: Mechanics
source: AP Physics C: Mechanics 2017 Practice Exam
text:
A cart of mass $M$ is on a level, horizontal track. The cart is attached to one end of an ideal spring, and the other end of the spring is attached to a vertical support. The spring has spring constant $k$. The cart is pulled to the right, stretching the spring a distance $d$, as shown above, and released from rest at time $t=0$. The cart-spring system begins to oscillate, and the position $x$ of the cart as a function of time $t$ is given by

$$x(t)=d\cos(\omega t),$$

where $x$ is in meters, $\omega$ is in $\mathrm{rad/s}$, and $t$ is in seconds. Assume the mass of the cart's wheels is negligible.

(a) Derive an equation for the velocity of the cart $v$ as a function of time $t$.

(b) On the axes below, sketch a graph of the velocity $v$ of the cart as a function of time $t$ for two complete oscillations. On the graph, $T$ represents the time for one complete oscillation. Explicitly label asymptotes, maxima, or minima with algebraic expressions, as appropriate.

(c) Express all answers in part (c) in terms of $\omega$, $t$, $M$, $d$, $k$, and physical constants, as appropriate.

  i. Determine an expression for the kinetic energy $K$ of the system as a function of time $t$.

  ii. Determine an expression for the potential energy $U$ of the system as a function of time $t$.

  iii. Using the equations from parts (c)(i) and (c)(ii), show that the rate of change of the total energy of the system, $dE/dt$, is zero.

Two springs identical to the spring above are attached to each side of the cart of unknown mass $M$, as shown in Figure 1 above. The cart is on a level, horizontal track of negligible friction. A piece of clay is added to the top of the cart so that it will stick to the cart. The cart is displaced to the left a distance $d$ and released from rest, as shown in Figure 2 above. The period of oscillation is recorded. This procedure is repeated for several different pieces of clay of different masses. In each trial, the cart is displaced the same distance $d$. The data are shown below on the linear graph of $T^2$ as a function of $m_C$, where $T$ is the period of oscillation and $m_C$ represents the mass of the clay.

(d) From the graph, determine the following.

  i. $k$, the spring constant of each spring

  ii. $M$, the mass of the cart

(e) The experiment is repeated, but in a second set of trials, the cart is pulled back a distance $D$, where $D>d$. Describe any changes that will occur for the straight line on the graph in part (d). Justify your answer.

points.description: Part (a): Award 1 point for indicating that velocity is the derivative of position, $v(t)=dx/dt$. Award 1 point for correctly taking the derivative, $v(t)=-\omega d\sin(\omega t)$.

Part (b): Award 1 point for labeling maximum and/or minimum velocity as $\omega d$ and/or $-\omega d$, or labels consistent with part (a). Award 1 point for beginning the graph at zero and in the negative direction. Award 1 point for drawing two sinusoidal cycles, with the first ending at $T$ and the second at $2T$.
Part (c)(i): Award 1 point for an expression consistent with part (a), $K(t)=\frac12Mv^2=\frac12M\omega^2d^2\sin^2(\omega t)$.
Part (c)(ii): Award 1 point for an expression consistent with the position function, $U(t)=\frac12kx^2=\frac12kd^2\cos^2(\omega t)$.

Part (c)(iii): Award 1 point for indicating that total energy is the sum $E(t)=K(t)+U(t)$. Award 1 point for correctly indicating that its derivative is zero, for example by using $k=M\omega^2$ so $E(t)=\frac12kd^2(\sin^2\omega t+\cos^2\omega t)=\frac12kd^2$, hence $dE/dt=0$.

Part (d)(i): Award 1 point for correctly calculating the slope using the best-fit line rather than data points, with acceptable slope about $0.48$ to $0.60\,\mathrm{s^2/kg}$. Award 1 point for relating the slope to the equivalent spring constant for the two-spring system. Since $T=2\pi\sqrt{M/(2k)}$, the slope is $4\pi^2/(2k)$, so $k=4\pi^2/(2\,\mathrm{slope})$; acceptable values are about $32.5$ to $41.5\,\mathrm{N/m}$.

Part (d)(ii): Award 1 point for relating the cart mass to the period and substituting values from a point on the graph. Award 1 point for substituting the spring constant from part (d)(i), yielding about $M=0.25\,\mathrm{kg}$.

Part (e): Award 1 point for indicating that the line would not change and 1 point for a correct justification. Since the period does not depend on amplitude for this ideal spring system, increasing the pullback from $d$ to $D$ does not change the graph. If the student does not indicate that the line would not change, the justification cannot earn credit.

subject: AP Physics C: Mechanics
source: AP Physics C: Mechanics 2017 Practice Exam
text:
A sphere of mass $m$ and radius $r$ is released from rest at the top of a curved track of height $H$. The sphere travels down the curved track and around a loop of radius $R$. The sphere rolls without slipping during the entire motion. Point $A$ on the loop is at height $R$, and point $B$ is at the top of the loop. The rotational inertia of the sphere is $\frac{2}{5}mr^2$. Express all answers in parts (a) through (d) in terms of $m$, $r$, $H$, $R$, and physical constants, as appropriate. Assume $r\ll R$.

(a) On the dots below, which represent the sphere, draw and label the forces, not components, that are exerted on the sphere at point $A$ and at point $B$, respectively. Each force must be represented by a distinct arrow starting on and pointing away from the dot.

(b)

  i. Derive an expression for the speed of the sphere at point $A$.

  ii. Derive an expression for the normal force the track exerts on the sphere at point $A$.

(c) Calculate the ratio of the rotational kinetic energy to the translational kinetic energy of the sphere at point $A$.

(d) The minimum release height necessary for the sphere to travel around the loop and not lose contact with the loop at point $B$ is $H_{\min}$. The sphere is replaced with a hoop of the same mass and radius. Will the value of $H_{\min}$ increase, decrease, or stay the same?

- [ ] Increase
- [ ] Decrease
- [ ] Stay the same

Justify your answer.

The sphere is again released from a known height $H$ and eventually leaves the track at point $C$, which is a height $R$ above the bottom of the loop, as shown in the figure above. The track makes an angle $\theta$ above the horizontal at point $C$. Express your answer in part (e) in terms of $m$, $r$, $H$, $R$, $\theta$, and physical constants, as appropriate.

(e) Calculate the maximum height above the bottom of the loop that the sphere will reach.
unit_name: Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems

topics.name: Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems

points.description: Part (a): Award 1 point for correctly drawing and labeling weight and normal force at point A; 1 point for correctly drawing and labeling weight and normal force at point B; and 1 point for correctly drawing and labeling the frictional force at point A. A maximum of 2 points can be earned if there are extraneous vectors.

Part (b)(i): Award 1 point for using conservation of energy for the rolling sphere, $mgH=mgR+\frac12mv^2+\frac12I\omega^2$, with $I=\frac25mr^2$ and $v=r\omega$. Award 1 point for the correct result $v_A=\sqrt{\frac{10}{7}g(H-R)}$.

Part (b)(ii): Award 1 point for correctly setting the normal force equal to the centripetal force at point A, $F_N=mv_A^2/R$. Award 1 point for an answer consistent with part (b)(i), $F_N=\frac{10mg(H-R)}{7R}$.

Part (c): Award 1 point for using the ratio of rotational to translational kinetic energies, $K_R/K_T=(\frac12I\omega^2)/(\frac12mv^2)$. With $I=\frac25mr^2$ and $v=r\omega$, award 1 point for the correct answer $K_R/K_T=2/5$.

Part (d): Award 1 point for selecting "Increase" or stating that the hoop has greater rotational inertia. Award 1 point for indicating that the greater rotational inertia reduces the hoop's linear speed for the same release height, so the hoop must start from a higher height to maintain contact through the loop.

Part (e): Award 1 point for using an appropriate kinematics equation for maximum height, such as $v_f^2=v_i^2+2a\Delta y$. Award 1 point for using only the vertical component of velocity at point C, $0=(v_C\sin\theta)^2+2(-g)\Delta y$. Award 1 point for indicating that the speed at point C is the same as the speed at point A. Award 1 point for the correct result $y_{\max}=R+\frac57(H-R)\sin^2\theta$. An equivalent energy solution that recognizes rotational kinetic energy is unchanged during projectile motion is acceptable.

subject: AP Physics C: Mechanics
source: AP Physics C: Mechanics 2018 Practice Exam
text:
A student sets up an experiment with a cart on a level horizontal track. The cart is attached with an elastic cord to a force sensor that is fixed in place on the left end of the track. A motion sensor is at the right end of the track, as shown in the figure above. The cart is given an initial speed of $v_0=2.0\,\mathrm{m/s}$ and moves with this constant speed until the elastic cord exerts a force on the cart. The motion of the cart is measured with the motion detector, and the force the elastic cord exerts on the cart is measured with the force sensor. Both sensors are set up so that the positive direction is to the left. The data recorded by both sensors are shown in the graphs of velocity as a function of time and force as a function of time below. The area under the force-time graph is $1.0\,\mathrm{N\cdot s}$.

(a) Calculate the mass $m$ of the cart.

For the time period from $0.50\,\mathrm{s}$ to $0.75\,\mathrm{s}$, the force $F$ the elastic cord exerts on the cart is given as a function of time $t$ by

$$F=A\sin(\omega t),$$

where $A=6.3\,\mathrm{N}$ and $\omega=12.6\,\mathrm{rad/s}$.

(b) Using the given equation, show that the area under the graph above is $1.0\,\mathrm{N\cdot s}$.

(c) The experiment is repeated using a different cord that exerts a larger average force on the cart. The cart starts and ends with the same speeds as those in the original experiment. Will the area for the graph of force as a function of time for the new cord be greater than, less than, or equal to the area for that of the original cord?

- [ ] Greater than
- [ ] Less than
- [ ] Equal to

Justify your answer.

The elastic cord from the original experiment can be modeled as an ideal spring with force constant $k$.

(d) Derive an expression for the maximum change in length $x_{\max}$ for the cord. Express your answer in terms of $m$, $k$, $v_0$, and physical constants, as appropriate.

The student performs several trials of the experiment. For the first trial, the cart is empty. In each succeeding trial, a block is added to the cart. In all trials, the cart has an initial speed of $2.0\,\mathrm{m/s}$ to the right, the cart rebounds to the left with a speed of $2.0\,\mathrm{m/s}$, and the maximum change in length of the elastic cord is measured. The total mass $M$ of the cart and the maximum change in length of the cord in each trial are recorded in the table below.

| Total mass of cart $M$ (kg) | $x_{\max}$ (m) |
|---:|---:|
| 0.25 | 0.142 |
| 0.75 | 0.253 |
| 1.25 | 0.349 |
| 1.75 | 0.431 |
| 2.25 | 0.465 |

(e) Indicate below which quantities should be graphed to yield a straight line with a slope that could be used to calculate a numerical value for the force constant of the elastic cord $k$.

Vertical axis:

Horizontal axis:

Use the remaining columns in the table on the previous page, as needed, to record any quantities that you indicated that are not given.

(f) Plot the data points for the quantities indicated in part (e) on the graph below. Clearly scale and label all axes, including units as appropriate. Draw a straight line that best represents the data.

(g) Use the best-fit line to calculate the force constant $k$ of the elastic cord.

points.description: Part (a): Award 1 point for correctly using the area under the force-time graph to calculate the cart's mass, using impulse $J=\Delta p=m(v_f-v_i)$. Award 1 point for the correct answer with units, $m=(1.0\,\mathrm{N\cdot s})/(2.0\,\mathrm{m/s}-(-2.0\,\mathrm{m/s}))=0.25\,\mathrm{kg}$.

Part (b): Award 1 point for relating the time integral of $F=A\sin(\omega t)$ to the area under the curve. Award 1 point for applying the correct limits: $J=\int_{0.50}^{0.75}A\sin(\omega t)\,dt=-(A/\omega)[\cos(\omega t)]_{0.50}^{0.75}=1.0\,\mathrm{N\cdot s}$.

Part (c): Award 1 point for selecting "Equal to" with an attempt at relevant justification. Award 1 point for a correct justification, such as the cart starting and ending with the same velocities, so the impulse and the area under the force-time graph are the same. If the wrong selection is made, the justification is ignored.
Part (d): Award 1 point for correctly using conservation of energy, $U_{\max}=K_{\max}$ or $\frac12kx_{\max}^2=\frac12mv_0^2$. Award 1 point for the correct expression $x_{\max}=v_0\sqrt{m/k}$.

Part (e): Award 1 point for correctly indicating two variables that yield a straight line usable to determine $k$. An acceptable example is vertical axis $x_{\max}$ and horizontal axis $\sqrt{M}$. Reversed axes or another valid linearization is acceptable.

Part (f): Award 1 point for a correct scale that uses more than half the grid; 1 point for correctly labeling axes with variables and units consistent with part (e); 1 point for correctly plotting data; and 1 point for drawing a straight line consistent with the plotted data. Reversed axes can earn full credit if consistent with part (e).

Part (g): Award 1 point for correctly calculating the slope from the best-fit line and not just data points, for example slope about $0.34\,\mathrm{m}/\sqrt{\mathrm{kg}}$; linear regression gives about $0.33\,\mathrm{m}/\sqrt{\mathrm{kg}}$. Award 1 point for relating slope to $k$, since $x_{\max}=v_0\sqrt{M/k}$, so $\mathrm{slope}=v_0/\sqrt{k}$ and $k=v_0^2/(\mathrm{slope})^2$, giving about $35\,\mathrm{N/m}$; linear regression gives about $36.7\,\mathrm{N/m}$.

content_blocks.text: A student sets up an experiment with a cart on a level horizontal track. The cart is attached with an elastic cord to a force sensor that is fixed in place on the left end of the track. A motion sensor is at the right end of the track, as shown in the figure above. The cart is given an initial speed of $v_0=2.0\,\mathrm{m/s}$ and moves with this constant speed until the elastic cord exerts a force on the cart. The motion of the cart is measured with the motion detector, and the force the elastic cord exerts on the cart is measured with the force sensor. Both sensors are set up so that the positive direction is to the left. The data recorded by both sensors are shown in the graphs of velocity as a function of time and force as a function of time below. The area under the force-time graph is $1.0\,\mathrm{N\cdot s}$.

content_blocks.label: (a)
content_blocks.text: Calculate the mass $m$ of the cart. For the time period from $0.50\,\mathrm{s}$ to $0.75\,\mathrm{s}$, the force $F$ the elastic cord exerts on the cart is given as a function of time $t$ by $$F=A\sin(\omega t),$$ where $A=6.3\,\mathrm{N}$ and $\omega=12.6\,\mathrm{rad/s}$.

content_blocks.label: (b)
content_blocks.text: Using the given equation, show that the area under the graph above is $1.0\,\mathrm{N\cdot s}$.

content_blocks.label: (c)
content_blocks.text: The experiment is repeated using a different cord that exerts a larger average force on the cart. The cart starts and ends with the same speeds as those in the original experiment. Will the area for the graph of force as a function of time for the new cord be greater than, less than, or equal to the area for that of the original cord? - [ ] Greater than - [ ] Less than - [ ] Equal to Justify your answer. The elastic cord from the original experiment can be modeled as an ideal spring with force constant $k$.

content_blocks.label: (d)
content_blocks.text: Derive an expression for the maximum change in length $x_{\max}$ for the cord. Express your answer in terms of $m$, $k$, $v_0$, and physical constants, as appropriate. The student performs several trials of the experiment. For the first trial, the cart is empty. In each succeeding trial, a block is added to the cart. In all trials, the cart has an initial speed of $2.0\,\mathrm{m/s}$ to the right, the cart rebounds to the left with a speed of $2.0\,\mathrm{m/s}$, and the maximum change in length of the elastic cord is measured. The total mass $M$ of the cart and the maximum change in length of the cord in each trial are recorded in the table below. | Total mass of cart $M$ (kg) | $x_{\max}$ (m) | |---:|---:| | 0.25 | 0.142 | | 0.75 | 0.253 | | 1.25 | 0.349 | | 1.75 | 0.431 | | 2.25 | 0.465 |

content_blocks.label: (e)
content_blocks.text: Indicate below which quantities should be graphed to yield a straight line with a slope that could be used to calculate a numerical value for the force constant of the elastic cord $k$. Vertical axis: Horizontal axis: Use the remaining columns in the table on the previous page, as needed, to record any quantities that you indicated that are not given.

content_blocks.label: (f)
content_blocks.text: Plot the data points for the quantities indicated in part (e) on the graph below. Clearly scale and label all axes, including units as appropriate. Draw a straight line that best represents the data.

content_blocks.label: (g)
content_blocks.text: Use the best-fit line to calculate the force constant $k$ of the elastic cord.
